stewart_read: Barbara names her son as co-owner on the title. The schema protocols.adult_child_decision_maker is explicit: the correct response is to capture the son's name and ensure he is included in the appointment. Parker says 'Your son. Okay. Perfect.' and moves on — no name captured, no inclusion confirmed. Strategic hypothesis: Parker is deferring son-inclusion to the booking step. Reactive hypothesis: Parker missed the signal. The reactive read holds — the booking step (04:07–04:52) also fails to confirm the son's presence, so no later move was made.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker intentionally deferred son-inclusion to the scheduling step to avoid friction mid-verify.
coaching_implication: Show Parker the schema protocols.adult_child_decision_maker entry. The correct move at 01:54 is: 'Got it — we'll want to make sure he's on the call with us too. What's his name?' This is a 10-second capture that protects the sit. Run the scenario: what happens Monday at 10AM if the son isn't available?

quote: Is that something you would be willing to trim back or cut down if it made more sense for your solar system?
stewart_read: Parker surfaces the tree proactively from satellite imagery and Barbara says she would 'consider' trimming or cutting it. This is a positive qualification signal — a customer willing to remove a shading obstacle is signaling real intent, not casual curiosity. Parker handles it adequately ('as long as you're willing to consider it, that's why we ask to at least get your design going') but doesn't anchor on it as a commitment signal or use it to deepen the interest conversation.
coaching_implication: This is a moment worth anchoring. Suggested rephrase direction: after Barbara says she'd consider it, Parker could tie it back to her stated motivation ('It makes sense') — 'Exactly, and if trimming that tree means the system pays for itself faster, that's worth knowing. Let's get you the design so you can see the actual numbers.' The tree-willingness is a buying signal; treat it as one.

quote: And then really the last thing, Barbara, I know you said your son does all the electric bill. The in order for us to build you a correct and accurate design, we do just look at your electric bill so you can see so we can see how much energy the home uses and how many panels you would need for the home.
stewart_read: Parker initiates bill collection correctly. When Barbara reveals she doesn't know the bill format and has only lived there a few months (03:26–03:47), Parker correctly identifies there is no usable bill history and pivots to booking at 03:55. The pivot decision is defensible — a customer with ~3 months of residency has no meaningful bill history to read back. The coaching failure is not the pivot itself but the absence of a handoff bridge: Parker never tells Barbara or the closer how the bill will be obtained. The closer will arrive Monday without bill data and no established expectation that they'll collect it on-site.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker should have continued pursuing the bill via the son or utility website.
coaching_implication: The pivot was correct. What's missing is the bridge: 'No problem — our specialist will pull that together with you and your son on Monday.' This sets the closer's expectation, keeps Barbara informed, and prevents the closer from walking in cold on a missing qualifier. Add this to the handoff brief coaching conversation.

quote: And then is the roof just a single roof there, an asphalt shingle roof?
stewart_read: Parker confirms roof type (asphalt shingle) but never asks roof age. The schema verify.roof_type_age requires both, with a hard DQ flag for roofs over 15 years. Barbara has only lived there a few months and may not know the roof age — but the question was never asked. A roof over 15 years old is a potential hard DQ or a roofing cross-sell opportunity; without the age, neither path can be evaluated.
coaching_implication: Add roof age to Parker's verify checklist. The question is simple: 'Do you know roughly how old the roof is?' If Barbara doesn't know, that's a flag for the closer to assess on-site. If the roof is old, it's either a DQ or a roofing cross-sell (schema cross_sell_signals.roof_replacement_planned). Either way, the closer needs to walk in knowing this is unresolved.

quote: Hey. Well, yeah, I can be quick, Barbara. I just work here with our design team just to get that started for you.
stewart_read: Parker uses neither the tesla intro nor the standard intro from the schema. There is no reference to a form submission, no Tesla framing, and no company legitimacy anchor beyond 'I'm with Ion' at 00:15. The low-pressure 'I can be quick' and 'design team' framing reduces resistance on a cold customer, which is a reasonable instinct, but it leaves the legitimacy question unanswered. Barbara never pushes back on who Ion is, so the gap didn't cost the call — but on a more skeptical customer it would.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker's informal framing was a deliberate energy-match choice for a cold, minimal customer.
coaching_implication: Note the positive: Parker's instinct to reduce pressure is right for this customer profile. The gap is that legitimacy ('you filled out a form' or 'Tesla referred your inquiry') was never established. For a cold customer who gives one-word answers, a single legitimacy anchor early reduces the risk of a mid-call 'wait, who are you again?' moment. Suggested direction: keep the low-pressure tone, add one legitimacy line — 'you had a request here' is already in the script at 00:18 and Parker used it, but it's thin without the form-fill or Tesla reference.

quote: probably either of them. Okay. Well, I'll get you down for a 10AM then. It seems a little bit more open.
stewart_read: Parker books 10AM Monday without completing the full button_up protocol. Missing elements: no one-hour block stated, no phone-appointment specification, no 'not driving, at a computer' anchor, and no 'is that fair?' verbal handshake. Most critically, the son's presence on the call is not confirmed — Barbara said she'd need to check with him. The appointment is booked on Barbara's tentative preference, not on a confirmed two-party commitment.
coaching_implication: Walk Parker through the button_up checklist item by item against this call. The 'is that fair?' handshake and the computer/not-driving anchor are the two highest-impact missing elements for no-show prevention. The son confirmation is the highest-impact missing element for sit qualification. A suggested close: 'Perfect — I'll put you and your son down for Monday at 10. The only thing we ask is that you're both not driving and can sit at a computer to review the design together. Does that work for both of you?' That single sentence covers three button_up requirements and forces the son-availability question.

quote: Just to qualify for each of those, you can have a credit score above 670. Is that sound like the case for you or your son?
stewart_read: Parker states the credit threshold at 670, which matches the schema exactly — unlike SESSION18_fcbf33c1 where the rep stated 650. This is a positive compliance signal worth noting. Parker also correctly extends the qualifier to include the son ('you or your son'), which is appropriate given the son's co-owner status. No coaching gap here; surface for the manager as a positive anchor.
coaching_implication: Positive anchor for the 1-on-1: Parker got the credit threshold right and extended it to the co-owner. Use this as a baseline — 'you did this correctly, here's what the same discipline looks like on the son-inclusion protocol.'

stewart_read: Barbara's answer to 'what got you looking at solar' is 'it makes sense' — a rational framing with no emotional content. The schema verify.interest_in_solar flags this as the emotional anchor of the call and notes that top setters spend 60+ seconds here. Parker responds 'Yeah. For sure.' and moves on. There is no follow-up question, no bill-pain probe, no attempt to find the emotional driver underneath the rational framing. The interest question is checked, not explored.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have read Barbara's cold tone as a signal to keep moving rather than probe deeper.
coaching_implication: This is the bill-anchoring setup moment. 'It makes sense' is an opening, not an answer. A single follow-up — 'What's been driving that? Is it the electric bill, or something else?' — could have surfaced bill pain that Parker could then flip (schema coaching_philosophy.objection_inversion). On a cold customer, one good emotional anchor early changes the energy of the whole call. Parker's instinct to keep moving is understandable; the coaching point is that this question is worth 30 more seconds even with a minimal customer.
